perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided that the Receiving Part y shall give the Disclosing Part y reasonable notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Company Data in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided that the Receiving Part y shall give the Disclosing Part y reasonable notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Company Data' in the exception clause introduces ambiguity. The contract defines 'Confidential Information' but not 'Company Data'. This makes it unclear whether the Receiving Party can disclose 'Company Data' under judicial order even if it falls under the definition of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Conf idential Information to any third parties for three (3) years following the date of its disclo sure by Disclosing Part y to Receiv ing Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as set out below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Company Data in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided that the Receiving Part y shall give the Disclosing Part y reasonable notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'Confidential Information' consistently, which is a defined term. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Company Data' in the exception clause. Because 'Company Data' is not defined, this creates ambiguity and a potential contradiction. The contradicted text references the modified exception clause, which now uses an undefined term. The definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction states that using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them leads to conflicting obligations, and that is exactly what happens here.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Confidential Information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Materials only to Receiving Part y's employees or bona-fidae consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or sh all execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable them to comp ly with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Sensitive Data, to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Materials only to Receiving Part y's employees or bona-fidae consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or sh all execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable them to comp ly with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Data' concerning the security precautions the Receiving Party needs to protect, we are creating confusion. 'Sensitive Data' is not defined, so it's unclear if the security precautions apply to all Confidential Information or only a subset, potentially weakening the protection of some Confidential Information. The contradiction lies in whether all 'Confidential Information' as defined in Section 1(a) is covered by this security clause, or if only some of it deemed 'Sensitive Data' is covered.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Sensitive Data, to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Materials only to Receiving Part y's employees or bona-fidae consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or sh all execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable them to comp ly with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Data' in the security precautions clause. Since 'Sensitive Data' is undefined, it's unclear if the security applies to all 'Confidential Information' as previously defined, potentially weakening the protection of some confidential information as per Section 1(a). The contradicted text and the Changed Text are located in the same place and it contradicts itself. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition because the change introduces a new, undefined term ('Sensitive Data') that conflicts with the established term ('Confidential Information'), creating conflicting obligations and uncertainty. This creates a strong contradiction as not all Confidential Information might be considered Sensitive Data.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall noti fy Disclosing Party immediat ely upon discovery of any unauthorised use or disclosure of Confidential Information or Confidential Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Pa rty, and will co-operate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials and prevent its further unauthorised use.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall noti fy Disclosing Party immediat ely upon discovery of any unauthorised use or disclosure of Proprietary Data or Confidential Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Pa rty, and will co-operate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials and prevent its further unauthorised use.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Data' in the notification clause creates ambiguity about what triggers the notification obligation. The contract defines 'Confidential Information', but not 'Proprietary Data'. This makes it unclear whether the Receiving Party must notify the Disclosing Party of unauthorized use of all 'Confidential Information,' or only 'Proprietary Data', which is undefined. This undermines the notification duty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) Receiving Party shall noti fy Disclosing Party immediat ely upon discovery of any unauthorised use or disclosure of Proprietary Data or Confidential Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Pa rty, and will co-operate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials and prevent its further unauthorised use.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in the notification clause. 'Proprietary Data' is not defined in the contract. This creates a contradiction because the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party is now unclear; it's uncertain whether they must report unauthorized use of all 'Confidential Information' (as originally intended) or only the undefined 'Proprietary Data'. This creates ambiguity in what triggers the notification obligation and directly conflicts with the original intent and enforceability of the notification clause, which related to Confidential Information.
